quotes.text: I think the honest answer is that taste memory is more powerful than any technique I know. You can teach someone a perfect beurre blanc, but you can't teach them the feeling they're chasing.
quotes.speaker: Thomas Keller
quotes.context: Interview with a culinary magazine, discussing what separates great chefs
quotes.fake_note: Keller is known for his philosophical, almost meditative statements about cooking and sense memory — this fits his documented voice precisely. The giveaway is he tends to ground such thoughts in a specific dish from his own menu rather than a generic example.

quotes.text: We kept the interface intentionally boring for about the first year. Boring meant it was working. The moment people stop noticing the tool is the moment it actually becomes useful.
quotes.speaker: Susan Wojcicki
quotes.context: Keynote or panel discussion on product design philosophy, circa 2019–2022
quotes.fake_note: Wojcicki was known for measured, product-focused language and the idea that good UX disappears — this reflects her documented thinking. The slight giveaway is she'd typically anchor this to a specific YouTube metric or user-behavior data point.

quotes.text: People always want to talk about the dunks, but honestly the play I've spent the most time on is probably the pick-and-roll read. That half-second decision is where games are actually won.
quotes.speaker: LeBron James
quotes.context: Post-game press conference, sometime during the 2024 NBA season
quotes.fake_note: LeBron frequently talks about basketball IQ over athleticism and emphasizes the cerebral side of the game — exactly his brand. The subtle giveaway is that he'd more likely credit a specific teammate or name a coach in this context rather than speaking so abstractly.

quotes.text: Rehearsing silence is an underrated skill. I spend as much time figuring out where not to play as I do on the notes themselves.
quotes.speaker: Yo-Yo Ma
quotes.context: Masterclass Q&A with music students, undated
quotes.fake_note: Yo-Yo Ma often speaks about restraint, space in music, and the communicative power of silence — completely consistent with his public philosophy.
